text: In September 2025, Mr. Zoo Kanek Abdul Rahim asked the minister responsible for the PDPA,
a simple question. When someone requests their own CCTV footage under the Personal Data Protection Act,
must the organisation preserve that footage while the request is being processed?
Minister Josephine Taylor's answer was clear. Yes, organisations must have appropriate measures
to preserve the requested data. There are criminal penalties for intentional destruction
to avoid and access requests. That was said in parliament on the 22nd of September 2025,
but by then, my footage had already been deleted. This is what happened when I made my request.
13th of April 2024, 5.06 in the morning, I was riding my motorcycle on Can Hill Road when a taxi cut into my lane. The taxi driver gave the first account,
he said I had skidded on my own. I had no memory to contradict him. The diagnosis came later,
a suck a raconoid hemorrhage. Bleeding around the brain, I had no memory of what happened to me.
Not the impact, not the minutes before, nothing. What I did know was this.
Two condominiums flanked the road where the accident happened. Both had CCTV cameras pointing
at the street. When security guards checked the footage, they identified it within minutes.
They described to me what had happened. I was then asked to make a formal request to
management. So I did, starting with the Scots Tower because that was the more direct front view
of the accident. The taxi cutting into my lane was on video. It existed. It was findable.
That footage was the only record of an event I have no memory of. I needed it for two reasons.
One, to understand what happened to me and to prove I did not skid on my own.
Two, to support and insurance claim against the driver. Both seemed straightforward.
Both were not. Four days after the accident, I walked into the management office of the Scots Tower.
I explained what happened. I asked for the CCTV footage of the road. They said no.
Privacy reasons. I asked who the data protection officer was.
They wouldn't tell me. I asked what company managed the building.
They wouldn't tell me that either. I asked who I could escalate to. No answer.
I left the building with no company name, no DPO contact, and no escalation path.
I had to identify the managing agent myself. I had to track down the DPO myself.
The other condominium, Suits at Cairnhill, began differently. Staff there had allowed me to take
a short recording of the CCTV screen. Not through the PDPA process. Just a brief recording
because they could see why I needed to understand what had happened.
After PDPC ignored my request for the more direct front footage from the Scots Tower, I later tried the other angle. I made a formal request to Suits at Cairnhill
for fuller footage. I did not say who had helped me earlier. I was trying to protect the staff.
The managing agent's DPO then refused in writing. On the 21st of May, 2024, he wrote,
Only by police direct or order the MCST to disclose the footage, the MCST is obliged to do so.
That is not what PDPC's own guidelines say. The guidelines for management cooperation say
access may not be limited only to enforcement agencies such as the police.
I wrote back citing those guidelines. The DPO's reply, It seems to me you are having difficulty
understanding our position. And he refused to communicate with me further. Neither condominium
cited a single ground from the law. The PDPA lists the only legal reasons a request can be
refused section 21, subsection 3, and the 5th schedule. Privacy as a blanket reason
is not on that list. Police only access is not on that list.
And later, the short recording from Suits at Cairnhill, the one I had taken only because staff helped me, was used by PDPC to say the footage was not personal data.
While I was trying to find out who managed the building, the clock was running.
At first I was told the footage was kept for many months. That was my understanding.
So when Night Frank later said no footage had been captured, I became alarmed.
Because I knew that was not true. Security guards had already identified the footage.
They had described what happened. That was when I began to suspect the problem was not that the
footage did not exist. The problem was that access could be denied until the footage disappeared.
A new management team later claimed the retention cycle was 20 to 30 days.
PDPC eventually said 17 days and accepted that figure without verifying it.
17 days. That was the window. And that date mattered.
PDPC later treated Night Frank's request to the MCST as the access request date.
Then it treated the auto deletion as happening before the MCST's former refusal.
Under that logic, session 22A became useless. The footage could disappear during the live
access process and deletion was still not treated as a breach.
I was still trying to find out the company name on day 4.
So I did what the law says to do. I filed a formal complaint with the Personal Data Protection
Commission. For the Scots Tower, I explained the verbal refusal on April 17. My written
follow-up on April 25, the missing company name, the missing DPO contact, and the claim that no footage had been captured. At that point, I did not know PDPC would later say
the footage had already been overwritten on April 30. What I did know was this.
The footage had been identified. Night Frank's no footage claim was false.
And I was worried that the Scots Tower MCST or Night Frank could deny access by letting the video disappear. I told PDPC that PDPC did not preserve or verify the more direct front
footage. Instead, I was told to go to the police. Only after that did I try the other angle from
Suits at Can Hill. When his managing agent refused on the police only ground, I filed that complaint
with PDPC as well. And then, nothing meaningful. No preservation action. No verification.
Instead, PDPC came up with reasons on behalf of the condominiums without checking the call facts.
And none of those reasons addressed what the condominiums had actually said.
The Scots Tower had said privacy, then no footage. Suits at Can Hill had said police only access.
Months passed. Eventually, there was a response. But not the one the law seemed to promise.
My member of parliament intervened. Twice. PDPC did not move. I appealed. PDPC did not change
its position. Not on privacy. Not on police only. Not on deletion. After I proved the data had been
deleted. And the police confirmed they had no video either. PDPC's only deflection had run out.
They had no other reasons to reject me. What they did instead was open some kind of investigation
into TST's data deletion. I do not know which obligation it was open under. I do not know what
they look at. I just know it was not the right I had asked about. That is the pattern. The right
to retrieve my own data was treated as not worth investigating. The deletion of that same data,
after it was gone, was treated as worth investigating. Eventually, PDPC issued its findings.
Two cases. Two sets of reasoning. For Suits at Can Hill, they said the CCTV footage did not
contain personal data. Here is how they made that determination. They relied on my own evidence.
A short hand phone recording of the CCTV screen taken at the management office after staff had helped me view what their camera captured. That recording existed because staff
helped me. I did not disclose that earlier help when I later made the formal request
because I did not want to get the staff into trouble. But later, PDPC used the recording
against me. PDPC said the rider was only a silhouette. No facial features.
No motorcycle license plate. Therefore, not personal data. But that is not what the statute says.
The PDPA does not say a face or license plate must be visible. It defines personal data as
data about an individual who can be identified from that data or from that data and other information.
And there was other information. The guards had identified the footage within minutes.
The request gave the date, time, place, and who I was. The whole reason I was asking was because
that camera captured my accident. Identifiability, not clarity. This is the moment to understand.
PDPC took my own evidence, used it against me. And the tests they applied, the clarity test,
does not exist in the legislation. It was invented by the regulator. To dismiss my case,
in football, there is a famous goal scored by Diego Maradona. His hand made contact with the ball.
The referee missed it. The goal stood. Afterwards, Maradona called it the hand of God. PDPC's
clarity test is the regulatory equivalent. They scored a goal with something never in the rules.
The legal test was identifiability. They replaced it with visual clarity.
Then they declared victory. And move on. Let me show you what PDPC did, side by side.
First, condominium suites at Cairnhill. The DPO said, only by Police Direct,
order the MCST to disclose the footage. PDPC said, the footage contains no personal data.
The condominium never said that. PDPC invented that reason for them.
Second condominium, the Scots Tower. I made a verbal request on April 17. I was refused on
privacy ground and given no DPO contact. No company name, no escalation path.
On April 25, I followed up in writing. When challenged, they later claimed no footage existed.
That was false PDPC's own decision says the footage existed until April 30.
But PDPC's preservation analysis did not give legal weight to either of those earlier events.
Not the April 17 verbal refusal by the managing agent. Not my April 25 written access request.
Instead, the timeline shifted to what happened inside the condominium.
The managing agent asked the MCST on April 29. The footage was overwritten on April 30.
The former refusal came on May 2. That is the twist. My request disappears.
The managing agent's earlier refusal disappears. The false no footage statement disappears.
The warning that the footage might be deleted disappears.
And S22A becomes useless because deletion before the former refusal becomes no breach.
In both cases, PDPC substituted its own reasoning for what the condominiums actually said.
And in neither case did they test the actual stated grounds against the law.
Section 21, subsection 3, together with the 5th schedule. These are the only provisions
that authorize an organization to lawfully refuse an access request.
Privacy is not listed. Police only is not listed. PDPC never even checked.
I wrote to PDPC with 9 questions. Specific legal questions across 6 sections of the PDPA.
Which section authorizes the clarity test? Why were the condominiums actual reasons
never tested against the 5th schedule? Why did the April 17 verbal refusal not matter?
Why did my April 25 written request not trigger preservation before the footage disappeared?
Why did many months, what security told me, become 17 days?

Why was a request handed by the managing agent treated as legally different from the MCST's own responsibility when section 4, subsection 2 removes most direct obligations from a data intermediary and section 4, subsection 3 puts the same obligation back on the organization.
As if it processed the data itself, those questions were never answered.
There was a repeated line. The guidelines are not determinative.
The PDPA takes precedence. But that does not answer the question.
It does not say which guideline. It does not say which section of the PDPA.
It does not say what the conflict is. And when PDPC did try to answer a limited point,
like the definition of personal data, they did not explain the original reasoning.
They introduced a new idea, reasonableness. As if identifiability depends on whether it
is reasonable to identify me from the footage. But the security guards had already found the
footage within minutes. They knew the date. They knew the time. They knew the location.
They knew who was asking. That is the real world identifiability that PDPC ignored.
The written findings were eventually released. When I posted questions on the reasoning, PDPC
did not answer on the merits. Months later, more reasonings appeared, including a reasonableness
glossed on identifiability without explaining the original gap. PDPC also declined to publish
the report online promptly. That only happened after I escalated. On June 23, 2025, PDPC wrote,
decisions and undertakings are generally published on the microsite within the year.
We are unable to commit to a fixed date at this point, so the public could not see the reasoning.
My questions were not answered on the merits. The findings were not promptly on the public
record. And PDPC treated the matter as closed, but the TST investigation had already proven that
the data had been deleted. That was no longer in dispute. Even so, the officer handling my case
continued with flawed reasonings for my suites complaint, closing it on the basis that it was similar to TST. That could not stand, TST was wrong. Saying suites was similar to a wrong decision
was not a reason at all. I asked for the decision to be reopened. What I got in return was rude
and unbecoming behaviour expected of a civil servant. I did not want to complain about PDPC.
I went there because I needed my footage. In September 2024, I escalated to IMDA.
I emailed Mr Liu Chunhong, IMDA's chief executive. I copied the PDPC commissioner,
and because I did not know which department would handle my feedback, I sent the email to three IMDA branches at the same time. The complaint ran for 11 months. I sent 12 emails.
The last was on the 14th of August 2025. During the IMDA investigation, my complaint was
leaked back to the PDPC officers under review. On the 20th of August 2025, Ms Yong Wan Ling,
IMDA's head of internal audit, wrote to me, her finding PDPC and its officers did not commit any wrongful practices. She endorsed PDPC's reasoning. She said PDPC had directed me to inform the police
to avoid interfering with police investigations. That was her stated rationale for why nothing was
wrong. That rationale does not exist in the PDPA. The only grounds for refusing access are section 21,
subsection 3, and the 5th schedule. Active police investigation is not listed. It was an excuse.
IMDA endorsed it. IMDA also said PDPC had acted in accordance with its protocols,
and it identified certain areas of improvement without naming them. The findings were final and
conclusive. The investigation was closed. By this point, I stopped asking whether my case
was handled wrongly. I started asking a different question. How many cases like mine
has PDPC ever found a bridge on? PDPC's website used to let you filter enforcement decisions
by obligation type. At some point, that filter disappeared. So I went through the entire register
myself, every decision, every finding. 384 cases compiled as of June 20, 2026. The access obligation
section 21 of the PDPA, the right of a citizen to access their own personal data, has produced zero bridge findings. Zero, across 384 published enforcement actions. Not one. The dominant pattern
of PDPC enforcement is data protection and consent organizations mishandling data they hold. The right
of a citizen to retrieve their own data from an organization, that is the least enforced provision in the entire register. Across those 384 cases, only two involve a complainant demanding access
to their own CCTV data and being refused. Both are mine. Both from the same accident
on Can Hill Road. Both dismissed without a finding that the access obligation was
breached. The clarity test explains this. If face or license plate must be visible for CCTV footage
to qualify as personal data and that standard exists nowhere in the statute, then any organization can deny an access request by asserting the footage is too unclear. And the complainant has
no tools to challenge that assertion. After the 1986 World Cup quarterfinal, Maradona was asked about
his goal. He said it was a little bit the hand of God and a little bit the head of Maradona.
Not quite admitting, not quite denying. PDPC's one line reply, the guidelines are not
determinative. The PDPA takes precedence to nine legal questions about the merits. Same energy,
they cannot say what really happened. So they give the same non-answer to each question
and declare the file closed. Here is what I have learned. Going through every document,
every decision, every response. Ordinary regulatory failure is uneven. Mistakes in different directions,
inconsistent outcomes, different responses to different challenges. What is documented here
across two PDPC cases? An IMDA review, nine legal questions. V-184 enforcement action
is not uneven. Every decision. At every level, in the same direction, PDPC invented reasoning for
the condominiums. IMDA stemmed no wrongful practices without opening the envelope. Nine
legal questions got one line. Zero bridge findings across 384 cases. This is the shape of a policy,
not necessarily an explicit one. Nobody needs to have written it down. Here is the principle
that distinguishes enforcement discretion from what happened here. Anybody can decide to actively
enforce something or not according to the circumstances of the day. That is a regulator's
discretion. What nobody can do is actively decide to deny the rights of a citizen or
resident and reinterpret the law to protect the perpetrators of that denial. That is what happened
here. An active decision to deny and an active decision to invent ground to justify the denial.
The clarity test is the legal mechanism. The zero bridge history is the outcome. The
non-engagement is what follows when a regulator cannot defend its position on the merits. I built
a site, pdpaaccessrights.sg. Every document is there. Both PDPC decision, the one that found
no personal data using my own handphone video, and the one that reframed a deleted footage case as an accountability bridge. The parliamentary answer from Minister Teoh promising preservation
and penalties next to what happened in my case. The nine legal questions and the same one line
reply. The enforcement registered analysis 384 cases. Zero access obligation bridge findings.
The timeline, the contradictions, the documented failures. Everything on that site comes from a
PDPC decision, the PDPA statute, a parliamentary record, or correspondence in my possession.
Nothing is asserted that it's not source. Every claim has a footnote. Every source is primary.
The evidence is public. You can check it yourself. I am not a lawyer. I am not an MP. I am a citizen
who asked for footage of an accident that erased his memory and found that the right the law promised me did not exist in practice. The access obligation has produced zero bridge findings
across over a decade of PDPC enforcement. Zero across 384 published cases. If a statutory right
has never been enforced, not once is it being enforced at all. And if not by what mechanism
and by whom is it being rendered unenforceable? All the evidence is at pdpaaccessrights.sg.
Check it yourself.
